stdout:
# Marketing: Text Syndicate — Second Draft Batch (X / Threads / note.com)

Per the role contract, this is a report only — I have not written or modified any repository files. The execution adapter is expected to persist this to `system/runtime/business_agents/text_syndicate/marketing/task-0002/latest.{json,md}`.

## Context read

Read `marketing/task-0001` and `market_research/task-0001` first. Task-0001 already drafted: a generic "which tool did you keep vs. cancel" reply-bait post, an "unpopular opinion" contrarian post, a "6 tools survived 30 days" X thread, a "replaced a human" Threads post, a "hot take on [specific task]" Threads post, and a note.com "6 AI tools that survived 30 days" article. This batch uses different angles and names different specific tools/tasks to avoid overlap, per task instructions. Niche, positioning, and platform format rules (no links in X post body, link-suppression finding, Threads 2-3x/day cadence, note.com disclosure requirement) all carry over unchanged from task-0001 and the underlying research — not re-litigated here.

**Audience for all drafts below (same as task-0001): early-career professionals, solo founders, and freelancers who use AI tools daily but are skeptical of hype.**

## X (Twitter) — audience: same as above, channel: X, no links in post body

1. **"I cancelled [Notion AI] after 3 months. Not because it's bad — because I found a $0 workflow that does 90% of what I was paying for. What's a paid AI feature you quietly replaced with a free one?"**
   Format: single post, cost-angle hook (distinct from task-0001's generic "which tool" question), reply-bait.

2. **"Ranking every AI meeting-notes tool I've actually paid for, worst to best: [Tool A] > [Tool B] > [Tool C]. Fight me in the replies."**
   Format: single post, ranked-list bait — invites disagreement replies, a different mechanic than the open-ended questions in task-0001.

3. **"The AI tool that saved me the most time this month wasn't a chatbot — it was [a specific automation tool, e.g., Zapier/Make AI step]. Nobody talks about the boring ones."**
   Format: single post, "boring/underrated tool" angle — distinct from the "hype vs. reality" framing already used.

4. **Long-form thread (up to 25k chars): "I gave [ChatGPT/Claude/Gemini] the exact same messy brief and graded the outputs. Here's which one I'd actually trust with client work."**
   Format: head-to-head comparison thread, single disclosed affiliate link only in the final reply — same link-placement rule as task-0001, but a comparison format rather than a "survived 30 days" retrospective, so it doesn't duplicate that thread.

5. **"Every 'AI tool stack' post is selling you 8 subscriptions. Here's the 2 I'd keep if I had to cancel everything else."**
   Format: single post, "minimalist stack" contrarian angle — different premise from task-0001's "unpopular opinion" post (that one attacked hype generally; this one is a specific "cut it down to 2" claim).

## Threads — audience: same core reader, channel: Threads, posted 2-3x/day per algorithm cadence

6. **"Free tier vs. paid tier: which AI tool actually justifies the upgrade for you? Naming one that's worth it and one that isn't."**
   Format: short post inviting two-part specific answers (worth it / not worth it) — a comparative framing distinct from task-0001's "replaced a human" and "hot take" posts.

7. **"What's the AI tool you use for something completely outside its intended purpose?"**
   Format: short "unconventional use" prompt designed for specific, screenshot-able replies; planned first-hour reply engagement in the 30-60 minute window per research's algorithm-velocity finding.

## note.com — audience: Japanese-reading side-hustle/AI-curious readers, channel: note.com, follow-up angle

**Title (draft, JP):** 「無料AIツールだけで、有料級の生産性は出せるのか？検証してみた」
*(EN gloss: "Can Free AI Tools Alone Match Paid-Tier Productivity? I Tested It")*

**Positioning relative to the already-drafted "6 tools survived 30 days" article:** that piece was a retrospective review of paid tools; this is a follow-up angle testing whether the free tiers of the same or adjacent tools hold up, which lets it link back to the first article as a series entry rather than repeating it.

**Outline:**
1. Intro — the previous article ranked which paid tools survived; this one asks whether you needed to pay at all (explicit callback for readers of the first piece)
2. Method — same tools/categories as the first article, tested on free tier only, same "used daily past week 2" bar
3. Section per tool: what breaks or gets capped on the free tier, and whether that limit actually matters for a typical solo/side-hustle workload
4. Verdict table — "free tier is enough" vs. "the paid upgrade earns its price," mapped to specific use cases (income-generating side-hustle work vs. casual use)
5. Affiliate disclosure block (アフィリエイトリンクを含みます) — links only for the tools where the paid upgrade is the honest recommendation; same live-terms verification caveat as task-0001 (note.com's non-Amazon ASP policy should be checked directly before publishing, not assumed from secondary sources)
6. Close — CTA to the X thread (#4 above) for the head-to-head model comparison, cross-promoting rather than duplicating content

---

**Note on scope**: same single-niche pilot as task-0001, no diversification. No content here has been posted or scheduled — these are drafts pending human approval via Approval Console and `finalize_content.py`.
